svg.text: Recurrence Analysis — Master Theorem at a Glance
a = number of subproblems, b = shrink factor, f(n) = combine cost
Case 1: f(n) = O(n^c) where c < log_b(a) → T(n) = Θ(n^log_b(a))
Case 2: f(n) = Θ(n^c) where c = log_b(a) → T(n) = Θ(n^c · log n)
Case 3: f(n) = Ω(n^c) where c > log_b(a) and regularity holds → T(n) = Θ(f(n))
Merge sort: a=2, b=2, f(n)=O(n) → Case 2 → Θ(n log n).
Binary search: a=1, b=2, f(n)=O(1) → Case 2 → Θ(log n).
• Strassen: a=7, b=2, f(n)=O(n²) → Case 1 → Θ(n^log₂7) ≈ Θ(n^2.81)
• Karatsuba: a=3, b=2 → Θ(n^log₂3) ≈ Θ(n^1.58) — beats schoolbook O(n²)
• Closest-pair: a=2, b=2, f(n)=O(n) → Θ(n log n) with smart strip combine
• Recursion depth = log_b(n); stack memory matters for large n
When recurrence is unbalanced (a·n/b unequal pieces), master theorem fails;
use Akra-Bazzi or recursion-tree summation instead.
Recognise the recurrence shape; the answer follows mechanically.
